summary: I was born in Nagano Prefecture on March 16, 1883. It is a rural area with cold winters and not as much snow as Niigata Prefecture, but the altitude made it quite chilly. My family lived on an average-sized farm there, and the village was peaceful and typical of the countryside. Growing up in Nagano shaped much of my early life and work ethic.

My mother was the only daughter of the Akiyama family, and my father was adopted into her family. She was a very serious person whom I respected deeply, and I believe I was influenced more by her than my father. The Akiyama family background shaped part of my upbringing and values, especially through my mother’s teachings about work and responsibility.

The Russo-Japanese War happened around the time I graduated from grammar school. My brother went to serve in the war, so I had to take over as foreman on our farm while he was away. This responsibility prevented me from continuing my education as I had hoped. I remember people in our village talking about the war and the victory, but I only knew about it vaguely.

I learned about Ninomiya Sontoku at school, as his story was popular in those days. Although there were hardly any books in my house, his teachings were part of the moral education we received. His example was something that influenced the way we thought about work and life in the countryside.

In my village, we observed the Bon Festival, which was not really a festival but a time when people stopped working to hold services for the dead. It was one of the few times the community gathered formally, and it was held once a year along with visiting the village shrine. This tradition was an important part of village life and our respect for ancestors.

We celebrated Tanabata, the Festival of the Weaver Star, by writing poems on narrow strips of paper. It was one of the traditional festivals in our village, and it brought some joy during the busy farming seasons. These cultural practices connected us to the wider customs of Japan even in our rural setting.

Most funerals in our country village were held in the Buddhist style, and Buddhism influenced many of our rituals related to death. However, Buddhist priests did not come to weddings or other ceremonies except funerals. This was different from what I heard happened in Tokyo or America, where Buddhist priests might attend weddings.

Our village had a tutelary deity and a shrine that we visited once a year for prayers by the priest. However, Shinto priests did not officiate weddings or other ceremonies in our farming community. Shinto practices were part of our annual festivals but less involved in personal family events compared to Buddhism.

I came to America in December 1906, encouraged by my brother and my sister’s husband who knew about the opportunities here. Although I did not know much about America before coming, I heard it was a good place to live and earn money. Upon arrival, I stayed in Seattle for about six months working at sawmills in places like Redmond and Keystone. America was a new and challenging world, but I was determined to work hard and pay back my passage.

Kenji Iguchi was a scholar of Chinese literature who ran a private school attended by many young men in my village during the winter. Although I did not attend his school, I heard about him and knew that he was influenced by Kanzo Uchimura. Many students from his school later came to America around 1906, which inspired some of us to consider going abroad.

Kanzo Uchimura influenced Kenji Iguchi, who taught many young men in our village about foreign countries and Christianity. Although I did not study directly under Uchimura or attend the school, his ideas reached us indirectly and contributed to the curiosity some of us had about America and Christianity. His influence was part of the intellectual background of that time.

I initially planned to leave Japan from Yokohama in December 1906, but I failed the physical examination there due to red eyes, possibly trachoma. I had to wait and stay at Rokkaku Hospital for two weeks with others who also failed the exam. Eventually, I left Japan from Yokohama on a different ship bound for Seattle via Victoria and Vancouver.

When I failed the eye examination at Yokohama, I stayed at Rokkaku Hospital for two weeks along with three other men. The hospital head guaranteed our passage if we traveled via Canada, which allowed us to continue our journey to America. This hospital stay was a necessary delay before I could embark on the ship to Seattle.

The ship I eventually took to Seattle was operated by the Nippon Yusen Line. Before that, I was supposed to be on a different ship from Yokohama but missed it due to the failed eye exam. The Nippon Yusen Line ship carried about 30 Japanese passengers, mostly young men like me, including a few from Nagano Prefecture.

We landed in Victoria, British Columbia, where we were told to stay for a week before continuing our journey. After the stay, we were given medicine and then traveled to America via Vancouver without trouble. Arriving safely in Victoria was a relief and an important step on my way to America.

From Victoria, we took a ship to Vancouver, where we boarded a train to enter America. There were Japanese hotels in Vancouver, and friends helped me navigate the unfamiliar place despite my poor English. Vancouver was a key transit point on my journey to America.

I arrived in Seattle and stayed there for about six months, working at sawmills in nearby towns like Redmond and Keystone. The Japanese community there was supportive, and I stayed in Japanese hotels where people helped me despite my lack of English. Seattle was my first home in America and where I began earning money to repay my passage.

One of the places I worked in America was a sawmill in Redmond, Washington. The work involved tasks like putting away sawdust, and most of the workers were Japanese like me. This job was part of my early efforts to earn money and establish myself in America.

I also worked at a sawmill in Keystone, Washington, alongside other Japanese workers. The work was hard but necessary to pay back the money borrowed for my passage to America. Keystone was one of the communities where Japanese immigrants like me found employment in the early 1900s.

Although I did not live in San Francisco, I heard that Buddhist priests might attend weddings there, unlike in rural Japan where they only came to funerals. This contrasted with the customs I knew from my village and showed me how traditions varied in big cities and abroad. San Francisco represented a different cultural environment for Japanese immigrants.

Los Angeles was mentioned as one of the big cities in Japan that young people might have longed for, but I did not have such ideas when I was in grade school. My focus was more on America as a whole rather than specific cities like Los Angeles. It was part of the broader awareness of foreign places that grew as I got older.

Smelza was one of the places where I worked or heard about in America, although details are sparse in my memory. It was part of the network of locations where Japanese immigrants found work in industries like sawmills and possibly smelting. These places were important for earning a living during my early years abroad.
